allocations.rationale: Gold is your safe lock against spikes in inflation. Over the last 5 years, it has grown about 25% on average, helping keep your money's buying power steady.
This is another gold fund, giving you double the exposure to gold so your money isn't just in one fund house.

Even though you are conservative, a little bit of good stocks (about one-third of your money) grows much better over time. The Nifty 50 is a basket of India's top 50 companies, which historically gives around 11% per year. Think of this as a 'good stocks' mix.

fund.name: Franklin India SHORT TERM INCOME PLAN - Direct - GROWTH

allocations.rationale: This fund invests in short-term government bonds and corporate bonds. It's like a very safe bank account but with a little bit more interest. Its returns have been around 5.5% to 6% in the last year.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This is a smarter 'safe option' that takes a bit more risk than the Short Term plan but gives better returns. It holds slightly longer bonds and historically has done around 10.5%.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on a low-cost app like Groww, Kuvera, Coin, or MFU since you prefer direct plans. Step 2: Link your bank UPI. Step 3: For the ₹36.7 lakh you have now, do a one-time lumpsum into two funds: ₹18,35,000 into the Short Term plan and ₹18,35,000 into the Nifty 50 Index fund. (This splits your 'safe' and 'growth' money right now.) Step 4: Set up Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) for all 5 funds starting next month. Split the ₹80,000 monthly SIP as: ₹12,000 each into the two Gold funds, ₹24,000 into the Nifty 50 fund, ₹24,000 into the Short Term plan, and ₹8,000 into the Medium Term plan.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Just check that all your 5 SIPs are running correctly and the amount is debiting from your bank. Nothing else needed.

After 1 year: This is your main review. Check if your total portfolio has moved away from the target mix. Since you are conservative, if the stocks (Nifty 50) have grown too much, move money back to the gold or bond funds. Similarly, if they have dropped and now feel too small, move a bit more into them during your monthly SIPs.
Every year: Only review once a year. Do not check every month because markets go up and down a lot in the short term, and that is normal for stocks.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year on your birthday (or any day you choose), check your total portfolio value. The plan is: 15% Gold ETF 1, 15% Gold ETF 2, 30% Nifty 50, 30% Short Term Bonds, 10% Medium Term Bonds. To keep it simple, if the Nifty 50 side has grown to more than 35% of your total, sell some and put it into the Gold or Short Term fund. If the Nifty has gone down and is now less than 25% of your total, sell the other safe funds and put the money into the Nifty 50. This keeps your mix steady and eventually helps you get back to your original allocation.
plan.rationale: You are 61 years old and want to protect your wealth for the next 5 years. Most of your money needs to be safe to handle the 'inheritance' responsibility. So we have put 65% of the money (₹52,000 out of ₹80,000) into debt funds (short term and medium term) which are very stable. We have also put 18% (₹14,400) into Gold, which is a great hedge against inflation and recession. Together, these 'safer' options (Debt + Gold) grow steadily. We kept just 15% (₹12,000) in the Nifty 50 Index fund because without stocks, the road to ₹1 crore (a common inheritance target) is very long. Since you don't have any liabilities and are in the New Tax Regime, we kept the portfolio simple to ensure low fees and steady growth. You have enough capacity to hold a small amount of equity without panicking during a market crash.
risks: Even though the money is mostly in safe debt funds, your total portfolio (Debt + Gold + Stocks) has some risk. If the stock market crashes, your 15% equity part can drop quickly.
Since you are in the New Regime (no 80C benefits), we avoided 'ELSS' tax-saving mutual funds because they are high risk for a short 5-year goal. The debt part works better for you.
Gold prices can be volatile in short periods. Do not sell gold if its price drops just because it dropped; wait a bit to see if it comes back up.
The 'Short Term' and 'Medium Term' funds are not Fixed Deposits. If interest rates (called yields) go up or down, the returns of these funds can change slightly.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: The plan scores highly on passive metrics for the equity sleeve (100% Nifty 50 Index) and heavy debt/gold allocation. However, the "Medium Term Plan" is an active closed-ended scheme (per AMFI code 119539) which acts as an "active" component in the debt portion, dragging the overall portfolio activeness score up (toward 0.0) despite the dominant index exposure. The plan is long-term oriented, emphasizes low fees, has no active research, and rebalances annually, resulting in a mixed but ultimately slightly passive profile.

pqs.reasoning: The plan shows low alignment because it includes significant gold (15% each of two funds) which is extremely unconventional for a standard wealth preservation plan and doesn't optimally serve the 'inheritance' goal in a liquid state; it also has an inappropriate 15% allocation to volatile large-cap equity (1/5th of portfolio going down-related risk) for a conservative investor at age 61 near a 5-year horizon; it contains minor internal inconsistencies where it claims equity is 'one-third' when it's actually 15%; however, it correctly avoids ELSS for a new-regime investor and selects low-cost direct funds/ETFs meeting cost preferences.
